g.data-narration: File systems are organized in layers. Each layer uses the services of lower layers. This modular design simplifies implementation and allows flexibility.
g.text: Layered organization from applications down to physical devices

g.data-narration: Top layer: application programs issue file system calls like open, read, write, close. They see a logical view of files and directories without knowing disk details.
Logical file system manages metadata and directory structure. It translates file names to file numbers. Maintains file control blocks containing file attributes and location data.
File organization module translates logical block addresses to physical block addresses. Manages free space. Knows which disk blocks are allocated to which files.
g.text: Logical-to-physical block mapping, free space mgmt

g.data-narration: Basic file system issues generic commands to device drivers. Manages memory buffers and caches for disk blocks. Handles physical block read and write operations.
g.text: Physical block I/O, buffer cache management

g.data-narration: IO control layer consists of device drivers and interrupt handlers. Translates high-level commands to hardware-specific instructions. Communicates directly with disk controller.
Bottom layer: physical devices - hard disk, SSD, or other storage. Data stored in sectors or pages. Transfer unit is a block, typically 512 bytes to 4 kilobytes.
The layered file system structure separates concerns. Each layer has a well-defined interface. This modularity allows different file systems to share lower layers.
g.text: Apps -> Logical FS -> File Org -> Basic FS -> I/O -> Devices
